segments.text: Cytokines are molecules that communicate among cells of the immune system

Properties of cytokines:
Small proteins, usually less than 30kd
Bind to cytokine specific receptors
Total number probably exceed 200
Cytokine actions

Interleukins
A group of cytokines secreted by leukocytes that primarily affect the growth and differentiation of various hematopoietic and immune system cells.

T-Cell activation
Signal 1
Interaction of antigenic peptide with TCR-CD3 complex
(Interaction of CD4 or CD8 with MHC)
Signal 2
Interaction with CD28 on T cell and members of the B7 family on antigen presenting cell
“Signal 3” Interleukin-2 expression
IL-2 expression
Overview of B-cell Development and Activation

Interleukin-2 (IL-2)
-Produced by activated Helper T-cells.  Also called T-Cell Growth Factor (TCGF)
-lymphocyte mitogen factor
-Stimulates growth and differentiation of T-cells, B-cells and Natural Killer cells
-Stimulates the production of antibodies
-15kd, variably glycosylated
-similar functionally to IL-15
-hematopoietin family cytokine

Recombinant human IL-2 (Proleukin) has shown preliminary success in restoring CD4+ T-cell levels in patients with AIDS. Side effects: flu like symptoms, shock, coma. Half-life is only 7-10 minutes with IV adminstration.

Interleukin 1 was discovered during the study of the pathogenesis of fever: pyrogen
Endogenous Pyrogens

Interleukin 1 (Endogenous pyrogen)
Exogenous pyrogens (PAMPS) are a trigger for innate immune response
Pathogen associated molecular patterns (PAMPS)
Pattern recognition receptors (PRRS)
In response, IL-1 is produced by Macrophages, Monocytes, Neutrophils (also Fibroblasts, Dendritic cells, Others….)

Blood/brain barrier
Tight Junctions
Pericytes
Asctrocyte end feet

Hypothalamus
OVLT releases prostaglandins
Endogenous Pyrogens

The hypothalamus is the thermostat of the body, in that it alters the temperature set point during temperature feedback and fevers. During a fever, the set point is raised, which causes the body to increase its temperature through both actively generating and retaining heat (vasoconstriction). If these measures are insufficient to make the blood temperature in the brain match the new setting in the hypothalamus, then shivering begins so those muscle movements produce more heat. When the fever stops (when PGE2 release ends), the temperature set point is lowered to normal, and the reverse of these processes (vasodilation, end of shivering, and nonshivering heat production) as well as sweating are used to cool the body to the new, lower setting.
NSAIDS

The major pro-inflammatory cytokines that are responsible for early responses are IL-1a, IL-6 TNFa.
Other pro-inflammatory mediators include, IFNg, TGF-b, GM-CSF, IL-11, IL-12, IL-17, IL-8

Tumor necrosis factor a (TNFa)
-a and b
- a  is also referred to as cachectin
-secreted by macrophages, mast cells, activated T-cells, NK cells 	neutrophils and fibroblasts.
-Part of killing ability
-induces apoptosis.
-Also causes fever and wasting,
-chemoattractant for neutrophils
-pro-inflammatory
-acts on hypothalamus to suppress appetite and induce fever
-systemic toxicity prevents it from being used as a generic anti-tumor 	drug

Interferons
-first cytokine discovered over 50 years ago
-influenza infected chick fibroblasts produced a protein that protected other cells from infection.  Also protected against infection from other viruses
Interferons block translation, promote RNA degradation, promote virus specific cytotoxic T-cell response and lysis of infected cells

Chemokines
chemotactic cytokines.
Structural studies shave shown that the cytokines characterized belong to one of six families:

Cytokines exhibit:
pleiotropy
redundancy
synergy
antagonism
cascade induction
The cells of the immune system are subject to control by a network of cytokine actions

Cytokine Storm-pathological secretion of extremely high levels of cytokines.
Sepsis (infection of the bloodstream) is the most common cause of death in US hospital intensive-care units and the 13th leading cause of death in the US. Overwhelming production of pro-inflammatory and fever-inducing cytokines such as IL-1b and TNF-a.
Cytokine storms are thought to be involved in deaths for several viral infections
Covid-19 can induce cytokine storm
IL-6 is thought to be the key player in the Covid-19 cytokine storm
Cytokines make you feel bad

Important Terms and Concepts
-Cytokines
-interleukins (IL-2 lymphocyte growth factor, IL-1 proinflammatory)
-NSAIDS
-TNFa, interferon
-chemokines
-Autocrine, paracrine, endocrine
-pleiotropic, redundant, synergistic, antagonistic, 	cascade induction
-cytokines make you feel bad
-Cytokine storm
Structural studies shave shown that the cytokines characterized belong to one of six families:

Chemokine
Any of several secreted low-molecular weight polypeptides that mediate chemotaxis for different leukocytes and regulate the expression and/or adhesiveness of leukocyte integrins

Interferons are used for treating human disease
Structural studies shave shown that the cytokines characterized belong to one of six families:

Soluble TNF-a receptor (Enbrel), and monoclonal antibodies against TNF-a (Remicade and Humira) have been used to treat rheumatoid arthritis in more than a million patients.
Anti TNF-a drugs reduce pro-inflamatory cytokine cascade and inflammatory response.  Drugs stop pain, stiffness, and joint swelling and promote healing and tissue repair.  Increased risk of infection and lymphoma.

3D crystal structure has been solved  for the three forms of the IL-2 receptor and ligand
Interferon Regulatory Factors

Interferon can induce synthesis of PKR
Inhibits translation by phosphorylation of transcription factor
Induction of cytokines
Interleukin-2 shares a common binding receptor with other interluekins

Major Cytokine Classes	Major Cytokine Receptor Classes
IL-1				Immunoglobulin superfamily
Hematopoietins		Hematopoietin receptor family
Interferons			Interferon receptor family
Tumor necrosis factors
Interleukin-17
Chemokines			Chemokine receptor family

Inflammation
Swelling (tumor), redness (rubor), heat (calor), pain (dolor)

Lymphokines
Outdated term applied to cytokines secreted by lymphocytes, especially TH cells
Monokines
Outdated term applied to cytokines secreted by monocytes and macrophages
Interleukin 1
